title: The Old, Old Story And The New Order
content:
آ آ آ آ They proved we could not think nor see,
آ آ آ آ They proved we could not write,
آ آ آ آ They proved we drank the day away
آ آ آ آ And raved through half the night.
آ آ آ آ They proved our stars were never up,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved our stars are set,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved we neï؟½er saw sorrowï؟½s cup,
آ آ آ آ And theyï؟½re not happy yet.

آ آ آ آ They proved that in the Southern Land
آ آ آ آ We all led vicious lives;
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved we starved our children, and,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved we beat our wives.
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved we never worked, and we
آ آ آ آ Were never out of debt;
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved us bad as we can be
آ آ آ آ And theyï؟½re not happy yet.

آ آ آ آ The Daily Press, with paltry power,
آ آ آ آ For reasons understood,
آ آ آ آ Have aye sought to belittle our
آ آ آ آ Unhappy brotherhood.
آ آ آ آ Because we fought in days like these,
آ آ آ آ Where rule the upper tens,
آ آ آ آ Because weï؟½d not write journalese,
آ آ آ آ Nor prostitute our pens.

آ آ آ آ They gave our rivals space to sneer,
آ آ آ آ Their mediocrities;
آ آ آ آ The drunkardï؟½s mind is pure and clear
آ آ آ آ Compared with minds like these.
آ آ آ آ They sought to damn with pitying praise
آ آ آ آ Or the cowardï؟½s unsigned sneer,
آ آ آ آ For honour in the ï؟½criticsï؟½ï؟½ ways
آ آ آ آ Had never virtue here.

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved our names shall not be known
آ آ آ آ A few short years ahead;
آ آ آ آ They hied them back through years of moan,
آ آ آ آ And damned our happy dead.
آ آ آ آ A newer tribe of scribes weï؟½ve got,
آ آ آ آ Exclusive and alone,
آ آ آ آ To prove our work was childish rot,
آ آ آ آ And none of it our own.

آ آ آ آ The cultured cads of First Gem cells,
آ آ آ آ Of Mansion, Lawn and Club,
آ آ آ آ Not fit to clean the busted boots
آ آ آ آ Of ï؟½Poets of the Pub.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ They prove the partners of the part,
آ آ آ آ The wholeness of the whole,
آ آ آ آ The gizzardness of gizzards, and
آ آ آ آ The Soulness of the Soul.

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved that all is nought, but there
آ آ آ آ Are things they cannot do,
آ آ آ آ The summer skies are just as fair
آ آ آ آ And just as brightly blue.
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve buried us with muddied shrouds,
آ آ آ آ When our strong hearts theyï؟½ve broke.
آ آ آ آ They canï؟½t bring down yon fleecy clouds
آ آ آ آ And make them factory smoke.

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved the simple bard a fool,
آ آ آ آ But still, for all their pains,
آ آ آ آ The children prattling home from school
آ آ آ آ Go tripping down the lanes.
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve proved that Love is lust or hate,
آ آ آ آ True marriage is no more,
آ آ آ آ But Jim and Mary at the gate
آ آ آ آ Are happy as of yore.

آ آ آ آ These insects seeking to unloose
آ آ آ آ The Bards of Sympathy!
آ آ آ آ Who strike with the sledge hammer force
آ آ آ آ Of their simplicity.
آ آ آ آ (They cannot turn the world about,
آ آ آ آ Nor damp the fatherï؟½s joy,
آ آ آ آ When some old doctor bustles out,
آ آ آ آ And nurse says ï؟½Itï؟½s a boy!ï؟½)

آ آ آ آ They want no God but many a god,
آ آ آ آ And many gods, and none,
آ آ آ آ The preacher by the upturned sod
آ آ آ آ Shall pray when all is done.
آ آ آ آ Amongst the great ï؟½twas aye the same,
آ آ آ آ The envious crawlerï؟½s part,
آ آ آ آ The lies that blackened Byronï؟½s name
آ آ آ آ And banished poor Brett Harte.

آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ve learnt in bitter schools to teach
آ آ آ آ Manï؟½s glory and his shame
آ آ آ آ Since Gordon walked along the beach
آ آ آ آ In search of bigger game.
آ آ آ آ Maybe, our talents weï؟½ve abused
آ آ آ آ At times, and neï؟½er been blind
آ آ آ آ Since Barcroft Boake went out and used
آ آ آ آ His stockwhip to be kind.

آ آ آ آ But laugh, my chums, in prose and rhyme,
آ آ آ آ And worry not at all,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½re insects whom the wheels of time
آ آ آ آ Shall crush exceeding small.
آ آ آ آ Have faith, my friends, who stand by me,
آ آ آ آ In spite of all the lies,
آ آ آ آ I tell you that a man shall die
آ آ آ آ On the day that Lawson dies.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
